title: The Other Side of the Map

paragraphs.en: Priya had been warned about culture shock, but nobody had told her it would happen in reverse. She had spent three years in Tokyo — managing the adjustment, the language, the unspoken rules — and had emerged, she thought, a more patient and perceptive person. Then she moved back to London, and everything fell apart.
paragraphs.fr: Priya avait été prévenue du choc culturel, mais personne ne lui avait dit qu'il se produirait à l'envers. Elle avait passé trois ans à Tokyo — gérant l'adaptation, la langue, les règles non-dites — et en était sortie, pensait-elle, une personne plus patiente et perspicace. Puis elle était rentrée à Londres, et tout s'était effondré.

paragraphs.en: The noise was the first thing. London had always been a loud city, but now it struck her as relentlessly, almost aggressively loud. The conversations on the Tube, the horns, the music from open windows — she found herself flinching at sounds she had grown up with and presumably never noticed. She wondered if this was what it felt like to be a stranger in your own home.
paragraphs.fr: Le bruit était la première chose. Londres avait toujours été une ville bruyante, mais maintenant elle lui semblait implacablement, presque agressivement bruyante. Les conversations dans le métro, les klaxons, la musique venant des fenêtres ouvertes — elle se surprit à sursauter à des sons avec lesquels elle avait grandi et qu'elle n'avait probablement jamais remarqués. Elle se demanda si c'est ce qu'on ressentait d'être un étranger dans sa propre maison.

paragraphs.en: Her friends, she realised, had changed — or perhaps she had. Either way, the fit was no longer quite right. Conversations that would once have felt natural now struck her as superficial; an obsession with property prices and weekend plans that she had once shared without question. She felt herself withdrawing, which alarmed her.
paragraphs.fr: Ses amis, réalisa-t-elle, avaient changé — ou peut-être elle-même. Quoi qu'il en soit, l'accord n'était plus tout à fait juste. Des conversations qui auraient semblé naturelles autrefois lui paraissaient maintenant superficielles ; une obsession pour les prix de l'immobilier et les plans du week-end qu'elle avait autrefois partagée sans question. Elle se sentit se retirer, ce qui l'alarma.

paragraphs.en: She described all of this to her Japanese colleague Hana, who had herself moved from Osaka to Berlin and back again. Hana's response was immediate and precise. 'You are not homesick for London,' she said. 'You are homesick for the person you were in Tokyo. That person only exists there.'
paragraphs.fr: Elle décrivit tout cela à sa collègue japonaise Hana, qui avait elle-même déménagé d'Osaka à Berlin et retour. La réponse de Hana fut immédiate et précise. « Tu n'as pas le mal du pays pour Londres, dit-elle. Tu as le mal du pays pour la personne que tu étais à Tokyo. Cette personne n'existe que là-bas. »

paragraphs.en: It was the most useful thing anyone had said to her. She had been approaching her unhappiness as a problem to be solved — something to push through, wait out, overcome. Hana's framing recast it entirely. You weren't failing to readjust. You were grieving.
paragraphs.fr: C'était la chose la plus utile que quiconque lui avait dite. Elle avait abordé son malheur comme un problème à résoudre — quelque chose à traverser, à attendre, à surmonter. Le cadrage de Hana le recast entièrement. Ce n'était pas un échec à se réadapter. C'était un deuil.

paragraphs.en: Over the following months, she began to treat her two selves — London-Priya and Tokyo-Priya — not as incompatible but as complementary. She stopped trying to become who she had been before she left, and started asking who she wanted to be now, drawing on both. This turned out to be considerably harder than adjusting to Japan had been. It also turned out to be more interesting.
paragraphs.fr: Au cours des mois suivants, elle commença à traiter ses deux moi — Priya-Londres et Priya-Tokyo — non pas comme incompatibles mais comme complémentaires. Elle cessa d'essayer de redevenir celle qu'elle était avant de partir, et commença à se demander qui elle voulait être maintenant, en puisant dans les deux. Cela s'avéra considérablement plus difficile que de s'adapter au Japon. Cela s'avéra aussi plus intéressant.

vocabulary.definition_en: dans la direction ou l'ordre opposé à l'habitude
vocabulary.definition_fr: à l'envers, dans le sens contraire
vocabulary.example: The same process happened in reverse when she returned home.

vocabulary.definition_en: ayant ou montrant une capacité à comprendre les choses rapidement et en profondeur
vocabulary.example: That's a very perceptive comment — you've identified the core issue.

vocabulary.definition_en: d'une manière qui ne s'arrête ou ne faiblit jamais ; impitoyablement
vocabulary.example: The sun beat down relentlessly all afternoon.

vocabulary.definition_en: faire un petit mouvement involontaire en réaction à la douleur, au choc ou à quelque chose de désagréable
vocabulary.example: She flinched at the sudden loud noise.

vocabulary.definition_en: manquant de profondeur ; ne s'intéressant qu'à l'évident ou au niveau de surface
vocabulary.example: The article gave a superficial overview without exploring the real issues.

vocabulary.definition_en: présenter ou penser quelque chose d'une façon différente ; donner une nouvelle forme à
vocabulary.example: Her therapist helped her recast her failures as learning experiences.

vocabulary.definition_en: ressentir une tristesse intense, surtout après une perte
vocabulary.definition_fr: faire son deuil, être en deuil de, souffrir d'une perte
vocabulary.example: It takes time to grieve the loss of a life you had planned.

vocabulary.definition_en: se combinant d'une façon qui améliore ou met en valeur les qualités de chacun
vocabulary.example: Their skills were complementary — where one was weak, the other was strong.

vocabulary.definition_en: utiliser les connaissances, l'expérience ou les ressources à votre disposition
vocabulary.definition_fr: puiser dans, s'appuyer sur, faire appel à
vocabulary.example: She drew on her years of experience to find a creative solution.

vocabulary.definition_en: se sentir triste et nostalgique d'un lieu ou d'une version de la vie laissée derrière soi
vocabulary.definition_fr: qui a le mal du pays, nostalgique (de)
vocabulary.example: He was homesick not for a place, but for a time in his life.

grammar_points.title: Either way / Either X or Y — expressing certainty despite uncertainty
grammar_points.explanation: **'Either way'** est utilisé quand deux conditions différentes mèneraient à la même conclusion. C'est un connecteur pragmatique qui montre que le locuteur n'a pas besoin de résoudre l'incertitude pour avancer.
examples: 'Her friends had changed — or perhaps she had. **Either way**, the fit was no longer right.'
'**Either way**, we need to make a decision by Friday.'
'**Either** she forgot, **or** she never knew. **Either way**, it doesn't matter now.'
Very common in spoken and written English at B2+.

grammar_points.title: Framing and reframing: 'approaching X as Y' / 'recasting it as Z'
grammar_points.explanation: Le langage du **recadrage cognitif** — changer la façon dont on interprète quelque chose — est très courant en anglais, tant dans le langage courant que dans des contextes plus formels (psychologie, affaires, journalisme).
examples: 'She had been **approaching** her unhappiness as a problem to be solved.'
'How you **frame** a question determines what kinds of answers are possible.'
Key verbs: 'approach', 'frame', 'reframe', 'recast', 'see X as', 'treat X as'

grammar_points.title: Noun phrases with 'something to + infinitive'
grammar_points.explanation: **'Something to be solved / waited out / overcome'** est une construction anglaise très efficace — un nom modifié par une phrase infinitive passive. Elle remplace des structures de propositions relatives plus longues.
examples: 'A problem **to be solved**.' → 'a problem that needs to be solved' (more concise)
'Something **to push through**.' → 'something you have to push through'
'Nothing **to worry about**.' → very common in reassurance
'A question **to be asked**.' / 'A lesson **to be learnt**.' (formal/written)

expressions.example: She had been warned about culture shock but hadn't expected reverse culture shock.
expressions.note: La désorientation psychologique en entrant dans un nouvel environnement culturel. 'Reverse culture shock' = le même sentiment en rentrant chez soi.

expressions.expression: the fit was no longer right
expressions.meaning_fr: ça ne cadrait plus, ça ne collait plus
expressions.example: She still liked her friends, but the fit was no longer quite right.
expressions.note: Utilise 'fit' comme métaphore vestimentaire — quelque chose qui vous allait parfaitement ne vous va plus.

expressions.expression: to push through / wait out / overcome
expressions.example: She had been trying to push through her unhappiness.
expressions.note: Trois expressions liées pour faire face aux difficultés. 'Push through' = traverser activement. 'Wait out' = endurer passivement. 'Overcome' = vaincre définitivement.

expressions.meaning_fr: en puisant dans, en s'appuyant sur
expressions.example: She built a new identity, drawing on both her London and Tokyo selves.
expressions.note: Une façon sophistiquée d'exprimer l'utilisation d'expériences ou de ressources accumulées.

expressions.expression: to be a stranger in one's own home / land
expressions.example: She wondered if this was what it felt like to be a stranger in your own home.
expressions.note: Expression idiomatique figée décrivant le sentiment d'aliénation par rapport à sa propre culture ou son environnement.

written.question: What does 'culture shock in reverse' mean in this story?
options: Priya experienced culture shock in Tokyo but not in London.
Priya felt disoriented returning to London after living in Tokyo.
Priya felt London's culture was superior to Tokyo's.
Priya decided to reverse her decision and return to Tokyo.
written.explanation: Reverse culture shock (also called 're-entry shock') is the disorientation experienced when returning to your home country after a long period abroad.

written.question: What is the key insight that Hana offers Priya?
options: That Priya should return to Tokyo as soon as possible.
That cultural adjustment always takes exactly three years.
That Priya misses the version of herself she was in Tokyo, not London itself.
That London has become a worse city than it used to be.
written.explanation: Hana says: 'You are not homesick for London. You are homesick for the person you were in Tokyo. That person only exists there.'

written.sentence: She had approached the problem as something ________ rather than something to be understood.
written.answer: to be solved / to be fixed
written.hint: A noun phrase with passive infinitive meaning 'that needs to be solved'.

written.sentence: He had years of experience in the field to ________ when planning the strategy.
written.hint: A phrasal verb meaning 'to use available knowledge or experience'.

written.sentence: She couldn't tell if she was angry or just exhausted. ________, she needed to rest.
written.hint: An expression meaning 'whatever the answer, the conclusion is the same'.

written.french: En rentrant chez elle après des années à l'étranger, elle se sentit étrangère dans sa propre ville.
written.suggested_answer: Returning home after years abroad, she felt like a stranger in her own city.

written.french: Ses amis avaient changé — ou peut-être elle. Dans tous les cas, la relation ne cadrait plus tout à fait.
written.suggested_answer: Her friends had changed — or perhaps she had. Either way, the relationship no longer quite fit.

written.french: Elle s'appuya sur ses deux expériences pour construire une identité nouvelle et plus riche.
written.suggested_answer: She drew on both experiences to build a new and richer identity.

oral.instruction: Read the paragraph about Hana's response. This is a moment of insight — Hana's lines should feel calm, precise, and slightly revelatory.
oral.text: Hana's response was immediate and precise. 'You are not homesick for London,' she said. 'You are homesick for the person you were in Tokyo. That person only exists there.'
oral.focus: Hana's lines are short and definitive. Don't rush them. The final sentence — 'That person only exists there' — should land with quiet weight.

oral.question: Have you ever felt like a stranger somewhere you should have felt at home — a place you grew up in, a social group, a family gathering? Describe the experience.
oral.suggested_answer: I experienced something like this when I visited my hometown after living abroad for two years. I had expected to slip back into familiar routines, but instead everything felt slightly off. The conversations I used to enjoy felt superficial; the places I loved felt smaller than I remembered. I think what I was mourning was a version of myself that had existed there — the person I was before I left. Hana's insight resonates: you can be homesick for yourself as much as for a place.

oral.question: Do you think living abroad changes people permanently? Is the change always positive?
oral.suggested_answer: I think living abroad does change people in lasting ways, though not always in the ways they expect. The most significant change is probably in perspective — once you've seen your own culture from the outside, you can never entirely go back to taking it for granted. That's usually positive: you become more questioning, more adaptable, more aware of how much of what you assumed was universal is actually local. But it can also create a kind of rootlessness — a feeling that you don't fully belong anywhere. The people who navigate this best, I think, are those who treat their different selves as complementary rather than competing.
